chapters.title: A sensory banquet
chapters.text:
Have you wondered what the world feels like to a dog? Why mosquitoes seek human blood? Whether a tick perceives color the way we do, or only shades of gray? What about taste, sound, and the senses beyond?

We will explore these questions through Umwelt — a German term for environment, introduced by Estonian biologist Jakob von Uexküll. An Umwelt is each creature's sensory bubble: the way an individual experiences surroundings shapes its entire perception of reality. Every species lives inside a different Umwelt, and they rarely overlap. Yet none of us feel limited, because it is all we have ever known — a convincing illusion that our senses capture everything worth knowing.

Stay curious and keep learning about the living world around you.

Humans rely on five primary senses: smell, taste, sight, touch, and hearing. But is that set universal across life on Earth? This summary reveals how animals perceive reality — and how differently that perception runs from our own. Let us begin.

Along the way you will see how much of nature remains invisible to human senses alone.

chapters.title: The world through a nose
chapters.text:
Dog owners may find this chapter especially illuminating. Smell is a dog's dominant way of navigating life — which explains those endless sniffing stops on walks. But how does canine olfaction actually work?

Humans exhale scent away when we breathe out. A dog's nostrils are engineered differently, with apostrophe-shaped openings that allow simultaneous inhalation during exhalation — the key to tracking a smell trail.

On walks, give your dog time to sniff.

Noses are not the only smell receptors. Ants detect chemicals through antennae, picking up pheromones from their environment. Pheromones are widely misunderstood as exclusively sexual signals from females. Whether functional human pheromones even exist remains unclear; evolution eliminated our vomeronasal organ, which processes such signals in other animals.

Pheromones are chemicals one member of a species releases to trigger a social response in another. Ants deploy them for trail marking, battle calls, and much more.

Snakes use their forked tongues — an organ we associate with taste — primarily for smelling. The tongue lacks taste buds yet gathers chemical information in stereo. Each fork samples opposite directions; stronger signals on one side steer the snake toward food or safety. Equal readings mean pressing forward until a scent appears.

Smell, in other words, can function as a full navigation system — one humans barely use compared with dogs, ants, and snakes.

chapters.title: Vision across species
chapters.text:
Odorant receptors trap airborne molecules to distinguish scents. Opsins — another class of protein receptors — capture light particles passing by, forming the basis of sight.

Among spiders, jumping spiders resemble humans most closely in visual ability. Most arachnids rely on touch, but jumpers depend heavily on eyesight, maintaining separate eye sets for detail and motion detection.

Starfish eyes suit their lifestyle perfectly, sitting at the tip of each arm. They detect large obstacles to avoid but neither color nor movement.

The first step to understanding another animal's Umwelt is to understand what it uses its senses for. ~ Ed Yong

Color vision depends on wavelength. Blue light spans 450–495 nanometers; green runs 495–570. Human opsins include three cone types absorbing long, medium, and short wavelengths — red, green, and blue.

Dogs and horses carry only two cone types, making them dichromats. Orange jump obstacles on racecourses are nearly invisible to horses; bright yellow, blue, or white markers stand out clearly. Many "color-blind" humans are dichromats too and should not be treated as disabled — yet our trichromatic culture can make it seem that way.

Treat unusual sensory abilities as windows into a wider world.

Did you know? In one Spanish province, 342 griffon vultures struck aircraft turbines over ten years. Their vision includes a blind spot directly above the head while they circle and scan downward for prey.

Vision, then, is not simply about sharpness. It is about what each species needs to detect in order to survive.

chapters.title: How animals experience pain
chapters.text:
Pain, like color, varies across species. Nociceptors — specialized neurons — generate the sensation. Their tips in skin and organs contain sensors that react to harmful stimuli.

A crucial distinction separates pain from nociception. Touch a hot kettle and nociceptors register the heat. Signals reach the brain, which responds with distress — that conscious suffering is pain. The two are linked yet distinct: nociception is brief; pain can linger.

Scientists once denied that animals felt pain or emotion. That view has shifted; mammals are now widely accepted as pain-capable. Researchers who study animal pain face ethical dilemmas when experiments require inflicting harm.

Rather than asking whether animals feel pain exactly as we do, it helps to ask why the sensation evolved — whether it aids survival, learning, or adaptation. That reframing opens a path toward understanding animal experience.

We may never fully grasp how intensely animals suffer, but respect and compassion remain essential. Welfare organizations fight unnecessary pain in laboratories and food production. Acknowledging animal suffering moves us toward a fairer world for all living creatures.

Consider how your actions might cause animals needless harm.

Recognizing animal pain is not sentimentalism. It is part of understanding how other creatures experience the world.

chapters.title: The power of touch
chapters.text:
Physical contact can trigger pain, but touch also binds a creature to its environment.

Human fingertips rank among nature's most sensitive touch organs. The star-nosed mole may surpass them. Its snout bears eleven pairs of finger-like rays packed with touch sensors forming an Eimer's organ — a tactile system for mapping surroundings. Pressing the star against tunnel walls or objects builds a three-dimensional model of the world.

Many animals lean on touch when light fails. Mallard beaks look tough and utilitarian, yet keratin surfaces — the same material in fingernails — hide dense touch receptors that guide feeding in murky water.

Whisking is another tactile strategy used by mice, rats, and opossums. Each whisker sits in a nerve-rich follicle sending data to the brain. Where humans swivel their eyes to inspect an object, mice sweep whiskers to chart nearby space. Cats and dogs do not whisk; humans and apes traded whiskers for dexterous hands.

Pause to appreciate touch — hug someone you care about.

For some species, touch means survival. Tiger wandering spiders coat their legs in thousands of ultra-sensitive hairs called trichobothria, eliminating the need for webs. The spider waits, detecting evening wind shifts, then senses the air disturbance of an approaching fly. The sensitivity is extraordinary — hairs register the pressure change of an insect in flight.

Touch can therefore operate as a precision instrument, not merely a background sensation.

chapters.title: Soundscapes beyond human hearing
chapters.text:
People long treated vibrations as visual phenomena, yet for many animals they belong to the auditory realm. Frequencies humans cannot hear are vital communication channels for treehoppers and crickets.

By giving in to our preconceptions, we miss what might be right in front of us. ~ Ed Yong

Owls demonstrate extraordinary night hunting through hearing. Human ears divide into outer, middle, and inner sections. Owl ears follow a similar layout, but the entire facial disc — feathers and enormous openings behind the eyes — functions as an outer ear, creating a radar-like system.

Birdsong carries its own science. An Australian zebra finch's melody pleases human ears, but do fellow finches hear it identically? Pitch, yes — speed, not necessarily.

In the 1960s Masakazu Konishi showed bird hearing operates on a faster timescale. Playing rapid click sequences to sparrows, he found neurons firing in perfect sync even when clicks arrived 1.3–2 milliseconds apart. Cat ears matched the rhythm only about 10% of the time under the same conditions.

Approach new information with curiosity and avoid jumping to conclusions.

Unlike touch, hearing works across great distances. Unlike vision, it functions in total darkness and penetrates barriers. Unlike surface vibrations, it needs no contact. Unlike smell, it travels at the speed of sound. These advantages explain why many species depend on hearing above all else.

What sounds silent to us may be a crowded soundscape for the animals living beside us.

chapters.title: Senses we cannot perceive
chapters.text:
Electric fish generated currents long before humans understood electricity. Roughly 350 species emit electromagnetic waves, advancing early electrical research. The electric eel reigns supreme — one species can discharge enough voltage to kill a horse.

Living in murky water with poor eyesight, eels weaponize electricity to locate prey through three modes:

1. Low voltage for electrolocation
2. Short, high voltage for hunting
3. Highest frequency for attack

Magnetoreception is another hidden sense. Turtles, lobsters, and many other animals navigate fog, deep water, and cloudy skies using Earth's magnetic field — critical for migration yet the least studied sensory ability.

Appreciate the diversity of life on Earth and work towards preserving it.

Sea turtles are master magnetic navigators. In 1991 Ken Lohmann found they possess two magnetic senses: one reading the angle at which field lines meet the surface (parallel at the equator, vertical at the poles), another gauging field intensity, which varies globally. Together these cues form a built-in GPS for ocean travel.

Did you know? Humans have more than five senses. Proprioception tracks body position; equilibrioception maintains balance.

Beyond the familiar five, countless species tap into electrical, magnetic, and other hidden channels we are only beginning to study.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Every sense matters equally to a living being. Animals cross-reference sensory input constantly, weighing what helps and what does not. We should adopt the same approach when trying to understand other creatures.

Studying animal senses means learning how they live — not merely how we might exploit them. Every organ, vibration, and subtle signal that seems insignificant to us can define an animal's entire world.

Try this


• Visit a nearby park and observe dogs or ants in the grass.
• Tour an inclusive museum that simulates the experience of visual impairment.
• Explore Marshmallow Laser Feast's project "In the Eyes of the Animal" to see forests through different species' eyes.

Read it till the end?

Every species lives inside its own sensory universe. Learning to see through their eyes widens ours.
